title: David Hart (UK political activist)

infobox.value: 5 January 2011 (aged 66)  [Bury St Edmunds](/wiki/Bury_St_Edmunds "Bury St Edmunds"), England

[Novelist](/wiki/Novelist "Novelist"), [film director](/wiki/Film_director "Film director"), [businessperson](/wiki/Businessperson "Businessperson"), [political advisor](/wiki/Political_advisor "Political advisor"), [political activist](/wiki/Political_activist "Political activist"), [property developer](/wiki/Property_developer "Property developer"), [farmer](/wiki/Farmer "Farmer"), [playwright](/wiki/Playwright "Playwright")

(4 February 1944 – 5 January 2011) was an English writer, businessman, and adviser to [Margaret Thatcher](/wiki/Margaret_Thatcher "Margaret Thatcher").[[1]](#cite_note-telegraph1-1) He also had a career in the 1960s as an [avant-garde filmmaker](/wiki/Avant-garde_film "Avant-garde film"). He was a controversial figure during the [1984–85 miners' strike](/wiki/1984%E2%80%9385_miners%27_strike "1984–85 miners' strike") and played a leading role in organising and funding the anti-strike campaign in the coalfields.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

(Redirected from [David Hart (UK political activist)](/w/index.php?title=David_Hart_(UK_political_activist)&redirect=no "David Hart (UK political activist)"))

British political activist (1944–2011)

| David Hart | |
| --- | --- |
|  | |
| Born | 4 February 1944  [Paddington](/wiki/Paddington "Paddington"), London, England |
| Died | 5 January 2011 (aged 66)  [Bury St Edmunds](/wiki/Bury_St_Edmunds "Bury St Edmunds"), England |
| Resting place | [Great Maplestead](/wiki/Great_Maplestead "Great Maplestead") |
| Alma mater | * [Eton College](/wiki/Eton_College "Eton College") |
| Occupation | [Novelist](/wiki/Novelist "Novelist"), [film director](/wiki/Film_director "Film director"), [businessperson](/wiki/Businessperson "Businessperson"), [political advisor](/wiki/Political_advisor "Political advisor"), [political activist](/wiki/Political_activist "Political activist"), [property developer](/wiki/Property_developer "Property developer"), [farmer](/wiki/Farmer "Farmer"), [playwright](/wiki/Playwright "Playwright") |
| Political party | [Conservative Party](/wiki/Conservative_Party_(UK) "Conservative Party (UK)") |
| [[edit on Wikidata](https://www.wikidata.org/wiki/Q5234767 "d:Q5234767")] | |

**David Hart** (4 February 1944 – 5 January 2011) was an English writer, businessman, and adviser to [Margaret Thatcher](/wiki/Margaret_Thatcher "Margaret Thatcher").[[1]](#cite_note-telegraph1-1) He also had a career in the 1960s as an [avant-garde filmmaker](/wiki/Avant-garde_film "Avant-garde film"). He was a controversial figure during the [1984–85 miners' strike](/wiki/1984%E2%80%9385_miners%27_strike "1984–85 miners' strike") and played a leading role in organising and funding the anti-strike campaign in the coalfields.

## Early life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=David_Hart_(political_activist)&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Early life")]

Born at [St Mary's Hospital](/wiki/St_Mary%27s_Hospital,_London "St Mary's Hospital, London") in [Paddington, London](/wiki/Paddington,_London "Paddington, London"), on 4 February 1944,[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-2) David Hart was the elder of the two sons of Anglo-Jewish businessman Louis Albert Hart,[[3]](#cite_note-telegraph2-3) the chairman/principal shareholder of the Henry Ansbacher [merchant bank](/wiki/Merchant_bank "Merchant bank"), which had been founded by Henry Ainsley [né](/wiki/Birth_name#Maiden_and_married_names "Birth name") Ansbacher.[[4]](#cite_note-Childs-4)

Hart was educated at [Eton](/wiki/Eton_College "Eton College") until his expulsion in his fourth year.[[4]](#cite_note-Childs-4) In the mid- to late 1960s, he made several [avant-garde](/wiki/Avant-garde "Avant-garde") films and was in the circle of [Bruce Robinson](/wiki/Bruce_Robinson "Bruce Robinson") (*[Withnail and I](/wiki/Withnail_and_I "Withnail and I")*). On *[A Game Called Scruggs](/w/index.php?title=A_Game_Called_Scruggs&action=edit&redlink=1 "A Game Called Scruggs (page does not exist)")* (1965) he worked with [Raoul Coutard](/wiki/Raoul_Coutard "Raoul Coutard"), regular cinematographer for [Jean-Luc Godard](/wiki/Jean-Luc_Godard "Jean-Luc Godard"), and was described by producer [Michael Deeley](/wiki/Michael_Deeley "Michael Deeley") as "the English Godard".[[5]](#cite_note-BFI-5)[*[failed verification](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")*]

By now, Hart had begun to work in property,[[3]](#cite_note-telegraph2-3) a field in which he became a millionaire by the late 1960s. Living extravagantly, he declared himself [bankrupt](/wiki/Bankrupt "Bankrupt") in 1974,[[6]](#cite_note-6) owing £960,000 by the time of the 1975 hearing,[[7]](#cite_note-Pearce-7) although this was discharged in 1978.[[8]](#cite_note-8) A later inheritance restored his fortunes, but he did not repay his earlier debts.[[3]](#cite_note-telegraph2-3)

## Political advisor

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=David_Hart_(political_activist)&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Political advisor")]

By the late 1970s, he was involved in [Conservative Party](/wiki/Conservative_Party_(UK) "Conservative Party (UK)") politics and the [Centre for Policy Studies](/wiki/Centre_for_Policy_Studies "Centre for Policy Studies") think-tank. He wrote speeches for [Archie Hamilton](/wiki/Archie_Hamilton "Archie Hamilton") MP, a friend from Eton.[[3]](#cite_note-telegraph2-3)

In the early 1980s Thatcher involved Hart in negotiations with the [Ronald Reagan US administration](/wiki/Ronald_Reagan_US_administration "Ronald Reagan US administration") regarding their "Star Wars" [Strategic Defense Initiative](/wiki/Strategic_Defense_Initiative "Strategic Defense Initiative").[[3]](#cite_note-telegraph2-3)

During the [miners' strike of 1984–85](/wiki/Miners%27_strike_of_1984%E2%80%9385 "Miners' strike of 1984–85") he was an unpaid advisor to Thatcher, the [National Coal Board](/wiki/National_Coal_Board "National Coal Board") and its chair [Ian MacGregor](/wiki/Ian_MacGregor "Ian MacGregor").[[9]](#cite_note-business.timesonline.co.uk-9) He was a controversial[[9]](#cite_note-business.timesonline.co.uk-9) figure during the miners' strike (the government distanced themselves from him as soon as his services were no longer needed)[[10]](#cite_note-10) and was instrumental in organising and funding the anti-strike campaign in the coalfields,[[11]](#cite_note-Marqusee-11) including funding a breakaway miners union, the [Union of Democratic Mineworkers](/wiki/Union_of_Democratic_Mineworkers "Union of Democratic Mineworkers") (UDM).[[12]](#cite_note-Clement-12) His involvement in aiding working miners extended to employing former members of the [SAS](/wiki/Special_Air_Service "Special Air Service") to protect the families of working miners[[13]](#cite_note-13) and using the resources of 'the secret state'.[[14]](#cite_note-14) Hart's involvement was eventually a source of bitterness for the UDM's leader [Roy Lynk](/wiki/Roy_Lynk "Roy Lynk").[[12]](#cite_note-Clement-12)

In 1987 he formed the [Campaign for a Free Britain](/wiki/Committee_for_a_Free_Britain "Committee for a Free Britain"),[[1]](#cite_note-telegraph1-1) "an extreme right wing organization", funded by [Rupert Murdoch](/wiki/Rupert_Murdoch "Rupert Murdoch"), which at one point called for "the [legalization of all drugs](/wiki/Drug_legalization "Drug legalization")",[[15]](#cite_note-15) and which had used "anti-gay material during their anti-Labour campaign [in 1987](/wiki/1987_United_Kingdom_general_election "1987 United Kingdom general election")".[[16]](#cite_note-16) In 1988 he played a leading role in mobilising young activists against pro-[devolution](/wiki/Devolution_in_the_United_Kingdom "Devolution in the United Kingdom") dissidents at the [Scottish Conservative and Unionist Party](/wiki/Scottish_Conservative_and_Unionist_Party "Scottish Conservative and Unionist Party") conference in [Perth, Scotland](/wiki/Perth,_Scotland "Perth, Scotland").[[17]](#cite_note-17)

Towards the end of [Hungarian socialism](/wiki/Hungarian_People%27s_Republic "Hungarian People's Republic"), Hart channelled support from the West to the fledgling [Fidesz](/wiki/Fidesz "Fidesz") party,[[18]](#cite_note-18) which at the time was an unofficial anti-Communist student movement developing at the [Eötvös Loránd University](/wiki/E%C3%B6tv%C3%B6s_Lor%C3%A1nd_University "Eötvös Loránd University") under the protection of the last Communist minister of the interior, [István Horváth](/wiki/Istv%C3%A1n_Horv%C3%A1th "István Horváth").[[19]](#cite_note-19)[[20]](#cite_note-20)[[21]](#cite_note-21)[[22]](#cite_note-22)[[23]](#cite_note-23) The group received a visit and material support from [George Soros](/wiki/George_Soros "George Soros") by 1985. It was formally founded in 1988, changed into a party in 1989, and by 1990 its members were part of Hungary's new parliament.[[24]](#cite_note-24)

In the autumn of 1993, he was appointed as a personal advisor to [Malcolm Rifkind](/wiki/Malcolm_Rifkind "Malcolm Rifkind"), [Secretary of State for Defence](/wiki/Secretary_of_State_for_Defence "Secretary of State for Defence"),[[25]](#cite_note-Kelsey-25) a position Hart retained when [Michael Portillo](/wiki/Michael_Portillo "Michael Portillo") succeeded Rifkind. Reportedly a long-standing Portillo contact, Hart is credited with writing the 'Who Dares Wins' conclusion to Portillo's 1995 [Conservative Party Conference](/wiki/Conservative_Party_Conference "Conservative Party Conference") speech.[[1]](#cite_note-telegraph1-1)[[26]](#cite_note-26) He was also involved in the 1995 plan to install 40 telephones and fax machines in a Lord North Street house for a Portillo leadership challenge to Conservative leader and prime minister [John Major](/wiki/John_Major "John Major") which never emerged.[[27]](#cite_note-27)

In the 2000s he was involved in the international [defence industry](/wiki/Defence_industry "Defence industry") – including being a lobbyist for [BAE Systems](/wiki/BAE_Systems "BAE Systems") and [Boeing](/wiki/Boeing "Boeing").[[28]](#cite_note-28) In 2004 an [arrest warrant](/wiki/Arrest_warrant "Arrest warrant") for Hart was issued concerning his alleged involvement in that year's [coup attempt](/wiki/2004_Equatorial_Guinea_coup_attempt "2004 Equatorial Guinea coup attempt") in Equatorial Guinea.[[1]](#cite_note-telegraph1-1) In 2007 *[The Guardian](/wiki/The_Guardian "The Guardian")* newspaper alleged Hart had received £13 million in secret payments from BAE,[[29]](#cite_note-LeighEvans-29) via Defence Consultancy Ltd, an anonymously registered company based in the [British Virgin Islands](/wiki/British_Virgin_Islands "British Virgin Islands"). While BAE was under investigation for [corruption at the time](/wiki/BAE_Systems#Corruption_investigations "BAE Systems"), Hart was not thought to have done anything illegal.[[29]](#cite_note-LeighEvans-29)

## Cultural depictions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=David_Hart_(political_activist)&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Cultural depictions")]

In 2004 the author [David Peace](/wiki/David_Peace "David Peace") published the novel *[GB84](/wiki/GB84 "GB84")*, a "fiction based on a fact" of the miners' strike. The book's most controversial feature was Stephen Sweet, who is referred to throughout by his driver as "The Jew", a vain and obsessive character allegedly based on Hart.[[11]](#cite_note-Marqusee-11)

However, in [Francis Beckett](/wiki/Francis_Beckett "Francis Beckett") and [David Henckes](/w/index.php?title=David_Henckes&action=edit&redlink=1 "David Henckes (page does not exist)")' study on the miners' strike, *Marching to the Fault Line*, Hart features more as light relief.[[30]](#cite_note-30) Hart is also portrayed as a central protagonist on the government's side in [Beth Steel](/wiki/Beth_Steel "Beth Steel")'s 2014 play *Wonderland*.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Hart himself wrote numerous plays, including *Victoriana*, *The Little Rabbi*, *The Ark & the Covenant*,[[3]](#cite_note-telegraph2-3) and two novels, *The Colonel* and *Come to the Edge*.

## Personal life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=David_Hart_(political_activist)&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Personal life")]

Hart lived in some style in [Suffolk](/wiki/Suffolk "Suffolk"); first at [Coldham Hall](/wiki/Coldham_Hall "Coldham Hall") (near [Stanningfield](/wiki/Stanningfield "Stanningfield")), [Bury St Edmunds](/wiki/Bury_St_Edmunds "Bury St Edmunds") and then at nearby Chadacre Hall in [Shimpling](/wiki/Shimpling "Shimpling").[[1]](#cite_note-telegraph1-1)

Hart was the father of five children, three sons and two daughters, by four women;[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-2) the four mothers were Christina Williams (whom he married on 21 October 1976), Karen Weis, Hazel O'Leary, and Kate Agazarian.[[1]](#cite_note-telegraph1-1) In an article for *[The Daily Telegraph](/wiki/The_Daily_Telegraph "The Daily Telegraph")* in June 2009, Hart revealed that he had been living with [primary lateral sclerosis](/wiki/Primary_lateral_sclerosis "Primary lateral sclerosis"), a form of [motor neurone disease](/wiki/Motor_neurone_disease "Motor neurone disease"), since 2003.[[31]](#cite_note-telegraph3-31) He died of [pneumonia](/wiki/Pneumonia "Pneumonia") at [West Suffolk Hospital](/wiki/West_Suffolk_Hospital "West Suffolk Hospital") in Bury St Edmunds on 5 January 2011,[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-2) aged 66,[[1]](#cite_note-telegraph1-1) and was buried at [Great Maplestead](/wiki/Great_Maplestead "Great Maplestead") in Essex on 17 January.[[2]](#cite_note-ODNB-2)

## Filmography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=David_Hart_(political_activist)&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Filmography")]

* *Sitting Quietly, Doing Nothing, Spring Comes and the Grass Grows by Itself* – [short film](/wiki/Short_film "Short film")
* *A Game Called Scruggs* (1965) – [featurette](/wiki/Featurette "Featurette") starring [Susannah York](/wiki/Susannah_York "Susannah York")
* *[Sleep Is Lovely](/wiki/Sleep_Is_Lovely "Sleep Is Lovely")* (a.k.a. *The Other People*, 1968) – [feature film](/wiki/Feature_film "Feature film")
